original_abstract:
The cycling of soil organic matter (SOM) by microorganisms is a fundamental yet poorly resolved component of the global carbon cycle. Current models suggest that a significant portion of SOM, particularly the biologically accessible water-extractable organic matter (WEOM), consists of small molecules derived from plant exudates and microbial lysates. However, the specific composition of these metabolites and the mechanisms governing their retention in soil—such as mineral adsorption—remain unclear.

In this study, we developed a robust untargeted metabolomics workflow using gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) to profile soil extracts. We evaluated various MS-compatible extraction buffers, including water, salt solutions ($K_2SO_4$, $NH_4HCO_3$), and organic solvents (methanol, isopropanol), both with and without prior chloroform vapor fumigation. Our analysis identified 55 metabolites, verified against authentic standards, including sugars, amino acids, and organic acids. As anticipated, chloroform fumigation significantly increased the diversity and abundance of detectable metabolites by lysing the microbial biomass.

To investigate the environmental availability of these compounds, we introduced a novel spike-recovery method using $^{13}$C-labeled bacterial lysates from *Pseudomonas stutzeri*. This approach allowed us to differentiate exogenous microbial metabolites from the endogenous soil pool. Results revealed that only 27% of the introduced metabolites showed high recovery ($>50\%$) in the WEOM fraction. Notably, cationic compounds (such as polyamines) and many anions exhibited less than 10% recovery, suggesting rapid sequestration through adsorption to mineral surfaces or clay particles.

These findings highlight that a substantial portion of the microbial "metabolome" may be physically inaccessible to the community, contributing to the long-term stabilization of SOM. This study provides a standardized methodological framework for soil exometabolomics and offers new insights into the chemical factors that regulate nutrient availability and carbon sequestration in terrestrial ecosystems.
humanized_abstract:
Microbial cycling of soil organic matter (SOM) is an important yet poorly quantified process within the global carbon cycle. Current theories of soil carbon biogeochemistry propose that a large fraction of SOM—specifically the biologically important water-extractable organic matter (WEOM)—is comprised of humus-derived plant exudates and microbial litter lysate metabolites. However, there is limited understanding of the identity and function of these metabolites, their fate, and the processes controlling mineral adsorption to soils.

We established a robust untargeted metabolomics approach using GC/MS for analysis of soil extract metabolites. We investigated the influence of different water-based MS compatible extraction solutions such as water, salts (K2SO4, NH4HCO3) and alcohols (MeOH, IPA) with and without chloroform fumigation. A total of 55 authentic standards for identified metabolites including sugars, amino acids and organic acids were used to validate our metabolome. The fumigation with chloroform for the elucidation of the alive microbial community and by releasing cellular metabolites after microbial biomass lysing was highly successful in increasing the range and intensity of detected metabolites.

The overall goal of this research is to understand the presence of engineered and microbial-produced quinolone and amide compounds in soils and their fate. As a first step to understanding their presence in the environment and prior to quantifying the microbial communities and expression of genes responsible for their bio-formation, we describe a new method, spike-recovery of 13C-labeled bacterial lysates of Pseudomonas stutzeri and assessed the environmental occurrence of microbial metabolites and heteratomic organic matter (HOM) in the water-extractable fraction of soil. Only 27% of the spiked microbial-derived metabolites yielded high recoveries (>50%) in the WEOM (water-extractable organic matter) fraction; less than 10% were recovered for most of the cationic compounds including the polyamines, as well as for most of the anions. Moreover, the majority of the metabolites were precipitated and sequestered immediately after spiking in soil by strong adsorption reactions occurring on clay minerals and soil particle surfaces.

We identified several thousands of chemicals and demonstrated that a large part of the microbial metabolome is shielded from the community resulting in a long-term stable Soil Organic Matter (SOM). We develop the field of soil exometabolomics by providing an exhaustive methodological set which will shed new light on soil carbon sequestration and nutrients cycling in soils.

document.output:
Microbial cycling of soil organic matter (SOM) is an important yet poorly quantified process within the global carbon cycle. Current theories of soil carbon biogeochemistry propose that a large fraction of SOM—specifically the biologically important water-extractable organic matter (WEOM)—is comprised of humus-derived plant exudates and microbial litter lysate metabolites.
